subject: science
title: Grade 6 Science
description: Cells, energy transfer, ecosystems, and evolution basics.

lessons.title: Introduction to Cells
lessons.objective: Understand what cells are and the differences between plant and animal cells.
lessons.content:
All living things are made of CELLS — the building blocks of life!

🔬 CELL PARTS (both plant and animal):
  • CELL MEMBRANE — thin outer layer, controls what goes in and out
  • CYTOPLASM — jelly-like substance where chemical reactions happen
  • NUCLEUS — the 'brain' of the cell, holds DNA instructions
  • MITOCHONDRIA — 'power stations' that release energy from food

🌿 PLANT CELLS have extra parts:
  • CELL WALL — rigid outer layer for support (made of cellulose)
  • CHLOROPLASTS — contain chlorophyll for photosynthesis (making food)
  • VACUOLE — large, stores water and nutrients

Some organisms are UNICELLULAR (one cell, like bacteria).
Humans are MULTICELLULAR (trillions of cells!).

exercises.question: Which cell part is called the 'brain' of the cell because it holds DNA?
exercises.hint: This is the control centre that contains genetic information.
exercises.explanation: The nucleus contains DNA (genetic instructions) and controls the cell's activities, like a brain controls the body!

exercises.question: Which structure is found in plant cells but NOT in animal cells?
exercises.hint: This is related to photosynthesis — making food from sunlight.
exercises.explanation: Chloroplasts are unique to plant cells. They contain chlorophyll which captures sunlight for photosynthesis.

lessons.title: Energy Transfer and Ecosystems
lessons.objective: Understand energy flow in ecosystems and food webs.
lessons.content:
ECOSYSTEMS are communities of living things interacting with their environment.

Energy flows through ecosystems:
  ☀️ Sun → Producers → Primary Consumers → Secondary Consumers → Top Predators

FOOD WEBS are more realistic than food chains:
  A fox might eat rabbits AND mice
  A rabbit might be eaten by foxes AND hawks
  Food webs show ALL the feeding relationships

ENERGY PYRAMID:
  Each level gets less energy (about 10% passes up)
  That's why there are fewer top predators than producers!

  🦁 Top predators (few)
  🦊 Secondary consumers
  🐰 Primary consumers
  🌿 Producers (many)

If one species is removed, it can cause a RIPPLE EFFECT through the whole ecosystem.

exercises.question: Why are there fewer top predators than producers in an ecosystem?
options: Energy is lost at each level
exercises.correctAnswer: Energy is lost at each level
exercises.hint: Think about the energy pyramid — only about 10% passes to the next level.
exercises.explanation: Only about 10% of energy passes from one trophic level to the next. The rest is used for life processes or lost as heat. That's why fewer organisms can be supported at the top!

exercises.question: A food ______ shows ALL the feeding relationships in an ecosystem. (one word)
exercises.hint: It's more complex than a food chain — it's a food...
exercises.explanation: A food WEB shows all the interconnected feeding relationships in an ecosystem, while a food chain shows just one path.
